Response: Google DeepMind (acquired October 2021); originally developed at University of Washington Movement Control Lab by Emanuel Todorov; commercialized by Roboti LLC

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: General-purpose physics simulator for research and development in robotics, biomechanics, graphics and animation, and machine learning requiring fast and accurate simulation of articulated structures

Response: C++, C, Python, other (C# for Unity plugin; Jupyter Notebook for tutorials)

Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Originally developed at University of Washington Movement Control Lab by Emanuel Todorov, Tom Erez, and Yuval Tassa (2012). Commercialized by Roboti LLC. Acquired by Google DeepMind in October 2021; made freely available then open-sourced under Apache-2.0 in May 2022. Industry-standard physics engine for reinforcement learning research.

Response: Extremely simple installation via pip. Single command installs everything including prebuilt binaries. No compilation required on Linux. Headless rendering supported out of the box via EGL/OSMesa, which is important for server-side RL training.

Response: Robust error handling via Python exceptions with clear messages. Invalid MJCF model files generate helpful parse errors indicating line and element. Handles missing textures and meshes gracefully with warnings.

Response: Excellent documentation and tutorial coverage. GitHub Discussions community is active and responsive. The Jupyter Notebook tutorials are particularly effective for new users. Assumes familiarity with Python and basic physics concepts.
